name: Basic Fondue (Fondue Neuchateloise)
recipeIngredient: 5 1/2 oz Emmental cheese and Gruyere cheese
recipeInstructions:
(Note: the above measurements are for each person.
Multiply by your number of guests.)
Grate and mix half and half.
This is Swiss cherry firewater: clear, dry-tasting -- not "cherry brandy", which is dark and sweet.
Most good liquor stores should carry it, at least one US brand like Hiram Walker or Bols. The best Kirsch is the Etter brand from Switzerland, but finding it is rare.
In Switzerland, fondue is usually prepared in a caquelon, an earthenware dish with a handle, glazed inside; any enamelled saucepan can be used, or a not too shallow fireproof dish.
Rub the inside of the pan with half a cut clove of garlic and let it dry until the rubbed places feel tacky. Place the wine in the dish and bring to a boil. Slowly start adding cheese to the boiling wine, stirring constantly until each piece is dissolved, then add more. When all the cheese is in, stir the kirsch into the cornstarch well, then add the mixture to the cheese and keep stirring over heat until it boils again. Add freshly ground pepper and nutmeg to taste.
Remove the dish to a small flame (Sterno or alcohol burner) and keep it bubbling slowly. Bread should be about 1-inch cubes for spearing with fondue forks and stirring around in the cheese.
Do not drink water with fondue; it reacts unkindly in your stomach with the cheese and bread. Dry white wine or tea are usual accompaniments.
Another tradition: the "coupe d'midi", or "shot in the middle", for when you get full: a thimbleful of Kirsch, knocked straight back in the middle of the meal, usually magically produces more room if you're feeling too full. Don't ask how this works; it just does.
The crusty bit that forms at the bottom of the pot as the cheese cooks is called the "crouton" and can be peeled off and shared among guests as a farewell to dinner.
